slides.title: What is a Proper Subset?
slides.text:
We learned that a subset is a part of a larger set.

A Proper Subset is a subset that is strictly smaller than the original set. It cannot be equal to the original set.

slides.title: The 'Improper' Subset
slides.text:
Did you know? Every set is a subset of itself!

If Set A = {1, 2}, then {1, 2} is a subset of A.
But it is NOT a Proper Subset. We call it an Improper Subset.

slides.title: The Logic
slides.text:
For a subset to be Proper, there must be at least one member left over in the main set.

Look at the diagram: Set B has members '3' and '4' that are NOT in A.

slides.title: The Formula
slides.text:
To calculate the number of Proper Subsets, we take the total subsets and subtract 1 (the improper one).

Proper Subsets = 2ⁿ - 1

slides.title: Example Calculation
slides.text:
Let's try Set P = { a, b }.
n = 2.

Total Subsets = 2² = 4.
Proper Subsets = 4 - 1 = 3.

slides.title: Listing Them
slides.text:
For Set {a, b}:

Proper: {a}, {b}, { }
Improper: {a, b} (We throw this one away!)
